other_observers: Tony Leukering, Brian Gibbons, Brandon Percival, Mymm Ackley. Others on following night.; I am the original finder.
duration: 5 minutes in morning, Heard calling over a 10 minute period at night.
specific_location: Roughly 300 yards east of the banding station.
description: On the morning of 20 May 2006, myself and a group of birders had been birding near the banding station at Chico Basin Ranch. As the sun started heating up the area and the birding became quiet, I wandered east through the Russian Olive grove in search of anything. Soon thereafter, I flushed a relatively large nightjar from the ground. The bird was dark brown overall, had long, somewhat rounded wings and a fairly long, rounded tail. What immediately caught my eye was that there were white corners to the rectrices. The bird wheeled and landed roughly 10 feet above the ground on a Crack Willow branch. When perched, I could see a large dark grayish head with a black median crown stripe and large dark eyes against a darkish rusty-brown overall face color. The bird was hunchbacked and deep chested. The throat was blackish and bordered posteriorly by a narrow white crescent. The upperparts were varied vermiculations of brown and gray. A bold gray slash was apparent across the scapulars, bordered by black. The underparts were light brown. When in flight, the outer wings were fairly rusty-brown. When perched, the tail, which extended well past the primary tips, were brown with black bars. I would say that overall, the bird was roughly 10 inches in size.  I tried to get a quick photograph of the bird, but the bird took off and flew into a dense thicket of Russian Olive. I then went back and retrieved the rest of the birders and we spent well over an hour trying to refind it, but could not. However, as I was pretty sure the bird would remain at that location, a few of us stayed until nightfall to attempt it to vocalize. Near dark, the bird began vocalizing its explosive whip-poor-will call for about a 10 minute period.Behaviors: Observed in flight, a weaving, fluttery flight close to the ground. Also observed perched horizontal on a tree limb with diagnostic large round head, hunch-back, and deep breast.Call: An emphatic and explosive Whip-poor-will, accented on the poor.Plumage: adult
